# Florian Ederer – Academic Website

Florian Ederer is Allen and Kelli Questrom Professor in Markets, Public Policy and Law at Boston University. His research spans industrial organization, contract theory, corporate finance, and behavioral economics, with a focus on innovation, competition, and information.

## Machine-Readable Papers

The following papers are available as full-text Markdown files for LLM consumption:

- [Bayesian Persuasion with Lie Detection](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/lie_detection.md) — Min and Ederer (forthcoming, Journal of Law and Economics). A sender chooses a signaling mechanism knowing the receiver can probabilistically detect lies; the paper characterizes optimal persuasion under imperfect lie detection.
- [A Tale of Two Networks: Common Ownership and Product Market Rivalry](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/tale_of_two_networks.md) — Ederer and Pellegrino (Review of Economic Studies, 2026). A general equilibrium model of oligopoly in which firms are connected through two large networks—product similarity and ownership overlap—finds that the welfare cost of common ownership, measured as deadweight loss, increased ninefold between 1995 and 2021.
- [Innovation: The Bright Side of Common Ownership?](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/bright_side.md) — Antón, Ederer, Giné, and Schmalz (Management Science, 2025). Common ownership increases innovation when technological spillovers are sufficiently large relative to product market spillovers; the sign and magnitude of the effect varies considerably across firms.
- [Anonymity and Identity Online](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/anonymity_identity_online.md) — Ederer, Goldsmith-Pinkham, and Jensen (R&R, Review of Economic Studies). Using only publicly available data, the SHA-1-based EJMR username scheme is reversed to recover IP addresses for millions of posts, revealing that toxic and misogynistic content comes from across the economics profession including elite institutions.
- [Digital (Killer?) Acquisitions](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/digital_killer_acquisitions.md) — Ederer, Seibel, and Simcoe (Working Paper). Examining 1,200 acquisitions by Amazon, Apple, Cisco, Facebook, Google, Intel, Microsoft, and Qualcomm, this paper finds that acquired patents receive substantially more citations post-acquisition and that innovation rises rather than falls in targeted technology areas, particularly when follow-on acquisitions occur; knowledge spillovers are driven in part by departing employees rather than retained ones.
- [Common Ownership Around the World](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/common_ownership_around_the_world.md) — Antón, Ederer, Giné, and Ramirez-Chiang (Working Paper). The first comprehensive global study of common ownership across 61,649 firms in 49 countries from 2005 to 2019; documents that average pairwise profit weights doubled in the median country and tripled in the US, that common ownership is three times higher among the largest firms, that the Big Three (BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street) account for the largest share of the global increase, and that common ownership is systematically shaped by antitrust laws, market entry regulations, labor protections, and investor protection across countries.
- [Disclosure and the Pace of Drug Development](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/disclosure.md) — Cunningham, Ederer, Hodgson, and Wang (Working Paper). Using the 2017 FDA Final Rule mandating clinical trial results disclosure, this paper shows that increased transparency leads to a significant slowdown in drug development: Phase 2 trial completion times rose by over 200 days, Phase 1-to-Phase 2 transition rates fell by more than 20 percentage points, and active trial sites in oncology trials declined by over 50%; the slowdowns are concentrated in drugs with more technologically related trials pending disclosure, consistent with firms free-riding on anticipated competitor information.
- [Killer Acquisitions](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/killer_acquisitions.md) — Cunningham, Ederer, and Ma (Journal of Political Economy, 2021). Using pharmaceutical industry data on 16,015 drug projects, this paper shows that incumbent firms acquire innovative targets and terminate their projects to preempt future competition; overlapping acquired projects are 23.4% less likely to be developed, these killer acquisitions are concentrated in low-competition markets and when acquirer patents are far from expiry, and conservative estimates indicate 5.3–7.4% of acquisitions are killer acquisitions.
- [Common Ownership, Competition, and Top Management Incentives](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/common_ownership_incentives.md) — Antón, Ederer, Giné, and Schmalz (Journal of Political Economy, 2023). A model of strategic product market competition with common ownership shows that common owners optimally provide weaker performance-sensitive incentives to top managers, leading to higher costs, higher prices, and less competitive product markets without requiring any communication between shareholders or managers; panel regressions and a difference-in-differences design document a 10–16% reduction in CEO wealth-performance sensitivity in firms with more common ownership.
- [Common Ownership: A Guide for Antitrust Practitioners](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/common_ownership_practitioners.md) — Ederer and Tecu (Antitrust Economics for Lawyers, Chapter 7, LexisNexis, 2025). A comprehensive survey of the theory, empirical evidence, mechanisms, and antitrust policy implications of common ownership, covering price and entry effects across industries, mechanisms (voting, managerial incentives, director interlocks), and implications for merger review and enforcement including the Texas v. BlackRock litigation.
- [Mergers and Acquisitions under Common Ownership](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/mergers_common_ownership.md) — Antón, Ederer, Giné, and Pellegrino (AEA Papers & Proceedings, 2023). Using a general equilibrium model with a hedonic demand system, this paper documents that U.S. public firm mergers are increasingly concentrated among pairs with high product similarity and high common ownership, and finds that rising common ownership dampens the profit-increasing effect of mergers while leaving most of the consumer surplus harm intact.
- [The Great Startup Sellout and the Rise of Oligopoly](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/startup_sellout.md) — Ederer and Pellegrino (AEA Papers & Proceedings, 2023). Documents a secular shift from IPOs to acquisitions of VC-backed startups, accompanied by a doubling of the entrant productivity premium and a sharp decline in the product market centrality of GAFAM, consistent with the hypothesis that startup acquisitions have contributed to rising oligopoly power in high-tech sectors.
- [Anonymous Attention and Abuse](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/anonymous_attention_abuse.md) — Ederer, Goldsmith-Pinkham, and Jensen (AEA Papers & Proceedings, 2025). Analyzes EJMR's evolving interactions with external information sources, documenting the prevalence of links to external domains, the surge in discussions driven by Twitter posts since 2018, and the categorization of individuals whose content is discussed on the platform.
- [Deception and Incentives: How Dishonesty Undermines Effort Provision](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/deception_incentives.md) — Ederer and Fehr (Working Paper, R&R Management Science). A dynamic tournament experiment shows that biased performance feedback by self-interested principals — despite being partially informative due to lying aversion — reduces average agent effort below the no-feedback benchmark, because strong discounting of the biased signal and persistent negative effects of being deceived erode incentives over time.
- [Common Ownership and Relative Performance Evaluation](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/common_ownership_rpe.md) — Antón, Ederer, Giné, and Schmalz (Working Paper). Shows theoretically and empirically that higher common ownership leads to lower pay-for-own-performance and higher pay-for-rival-performance in executive compensation, with causal evidence from the 2003 mutual fund trading scandal supporting the interpretation that common ownership distorts managerial incentives away from aggressive competition.
- [Incentives for Parallel Innovation](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/parallel_innovation.md) — Ederer (Working Paper, R&R Journal of Law, Economics, and Organization). When innovators can learn from each other's exploration, free-riding leads to under-exploration under standard pay-for-performance; optimal contracts for parallel innovation combine individual and group incentives rewarding long-term joint success, a result confirmed in a laboratory experiment.
- [Trust and Promises over Time](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/trust_promises.md) — Ederer and Schneider (AEJ Microeconomics, 2022). A 2×3 laboratory experiment finds that communication raises cooperation rates by approximately 50% in a trust game, and that this effect is remarkably stable over delays of up to three weeks, with explicit promises driving most of the cooperation increase.
- [Understanding Mechanisms Underlying Peer Effects: Evidence from a Field Experiment on Financial Decisions](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/peer_effects.md) — Bursztyn, Ederer, Ferman, and Yuchtman (Econometrica, 2014). A double-randomization design with 150 pairs of socially connected Brazilian bank clients separately identifies social learning (29 percentage point effect) and social utility (22 percentage point effect) as independent channels of peer influence on financial product take-up.
- [Is Pay-for-Performance Detrimental to Innovation?](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/pay_for_performance_innovation.md) — Ederer and Manso (Management Science, 2013). Two laboratory experiments using gold prospecting and lemonade stand tasks show that exploration contracts — which tolerate early failure and reward long-run success — substantially outperform standard pay-for-performance in eliciting innovative behavior.
- [Delay and Deadlines: Freeriding and Information Revelation in Partnerships](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/delay_deadlines.md) — Campbell, Ederer, and Spinnewijn (AEJ Microeconomics, 2014). In partnerships with joint decisions, agents strategically conceal acquired information to induce free-riding by their partners; tighter deadlines can paradoxically increase expected decision time and quality, and under optimally chosen deadlines, private information dominates public information — a manifestation of Parkinson's Law.
- [Gaming and Strategic Opacity in Incentive Provision](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/gaming_strategic_opacity.md) — Ederer, Holden, and Meyer (RAND Journal of Economics, 2018). When agents have private information about the relative costs of performing two complementary tasks, strategic opacity through ex post randomization reduces gaming and dominates both ex ante randomization and deterministic contracts when task complementarity is sufficiently high.
- [Promises and Expectations](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/promises_expectations.md) — Ederer and Stremitzer (Games and Economic Behavior, 2017). A laboratory experiment manipulating promisors' second-order expectations identifies guilt aversion as the mechanism driving promise-keeping: higher anticipated expectations lead to significantly more generous promisor behavior, consistent with a conditional guilt aversion model.
- [Moral Intuitions of Promise Keeping](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/moral_intuitions.md) — Ederer and Stremitzer (Principia, 2018). Experimental evidence shows that lay moral intuitions about promise-keeping — including the binding force of mutual promises, the persistence of obligation over time, and the distinction between continuous and discrete performance — closely track established contract law doctrines.
- [Search Fatigue](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/search_fatigue.md) — Carlin and Ederer (Review of Industrial Organization, 2019). When consumer search is not only costly but tiring, markets exhibit cyclical prices, strategic product proliferation, and voluntary consumer assistance as firm responses to exploit or attract fatigued searchers, generating welfare losses that do not arise in standard search models.
- [Interpersonal Comparison, Status and Ambition in Organizations](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/status_ambition.md) — Ederer and Patacconi (Journal of Economic Behavior and Organization, 2010). A tournament model with reference-dependent status concerns shows that promotion-based tournament formats dominate demotion-based ones because status gains from promotion are cheaper for the firm to provide than compensation for status losses from demotion; ambitious workers exert higher effort but can discourage non-ambitious rivals in mixed tournaments.
- [Feedback and Motivation in Dynamic Tournaments](https://florianederer.github.io/papers/feedback_motivation.md) — Ederer (Journal of Economics and Management Strategy, 2010). Interim performance feedback in two-stage tournaments triggers competing evaluation and motivation effects; whether feedback raises or lowers total expected effort depends on the distribution of performance gaps and the complementarity between ability and effort, and the optimal feedback policy trades off total effort against selection efficiency.
